title: Minestrone
subtitle: The Italian everything soup, thick with vegetables and pasta.
excerpt: A dense, nourishing Italian broth loaded with seasonal vegetables, cannellini beans and small pasta, finished with a rind of Parmesan and a splash of olive oil. Deeply adaptable: use whatever the week has produced. Serves six.
sourceNotes: Minestrone. The name covers a broad family of Italian vegetable soups that vary by region and season. The Milanese version includes rice; the Genovese adds pesto at the end; this recipe uses a pan-Italian base. References to minestra appear throughout Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, public domain).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the pasta for up to three months. Add freshly cooked pasta when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The soup keeps for five days in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the base the day before; the vegetables and beans develop more flavour overnight. Cook and add pasta just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Italian aromatic base of finely chopped onion, celery and carrot cooked slowly in olive oil. The foundation flavour for soups, braises and sauces.

content.text: Minestrone is a way of thinking about soup more than a recipe: a soffritto base, seasonal vegetables cut to roughly the same size, a tin of beans, stock, and pasta or rice. The Parmesan rind simmered in the broth for an hour gives a savoury depth you cannot replicate with a seasoning shortcut. Use it. The rind costs nothing if you keep them in a bag in the freezer.

items.prepNote: small pasta such as ditalini or small macaroni
one rind to simmer, plus grated to serve

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large pot over low-medium heat. Add {{onion}} diced onion, {{celery}} diced celery and {{carrot}} diced carrot. Cook gently for 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft but not coloured. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook for 2 more minutes.

Add {{potato}} diced potato, {{courgette}} diced courgette, {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{cannellini-beans}} of beans and {{stock-vegetable}} of stock. Drop in the Parmesan rind. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Bring to a simmer and cook for 25 minutes until the potatoes are tender.

Add {{pasta-dried}} of small pasta to the pot. If using kale, add it now too. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes until the pasta is just tender. Remove the Parmesan rind. Taste and adjust seasoning. Ladle into deep bowls and finish with a drizzle of extra-virgin olive oil and grated Parmesan.

items.symptom: Pasta turned to mush in leftovers
items.cause: Pasta absorbed liquid and overcame as it sat in the soup.
items.fix: Cook pasta separately and add it to each bowl at serving. The soup base keeps better this way.

items.cause: Too much stock, or vegetables cut too large to dissolve starch.
items.fix: Mash a ladleful of the beans against the side of the pot and stir back in; it thickens the broth naturally.

items.cause: No Parmesan rind, or soffritto undercooked.
items.fix: Add a rind and simmer an extra 15 minutes. The soffritto needs 12 full minutes to develop sweetness.

content.text: Minestrone is the Italian thrift soup: a meal built from the vegetable drawer and the store cupboard. Every Italian region has a version; every Italian family has adjustments. It is one of those dishes that tastes better the second day, when the flavours have had time to settle. Serve in winter with a thick slice of bread, or in summer with a spoonful of pesto stirred in at the end.
